pillars.title: Engineering
pillars.teaser: Since 2009, my work has been about one thing: building systems that don't just function — they endure. From microcontrollers in a Cairo lab to safety-critical ADAS at WABCO (ZF), engineering isn't what I do. It's how I think.

modal.html: Since childhood, my answer to "what do you want to be when you grow up?" was unconventional: "I want to be a God." Not out of ego — out of a fascination with autonomous intelligent systems. The moment I brought my first microcontroller to life in undergrad, my mission was set: building intelligent systems that protect people. Today, I lead R&D for commercial vehicle ADAS — designing systems that save lives.

Since 2009, I have evolved a core belief: I don't just lead teams — I build robust development architectures. My approach is rooted in the V-Model of both software and people, ensuring scalability is baked in from day one. I act as the high-bandwidth bridge between strategic vision and the tactical reality of the engineering floor — and I never ask for an output I don't fundamentally understand.

modal.text: A leader must remain functional in the field. The moment you stop understanding what your team builds, you stop being an engineer — and start being a passenger.

columns: 30% faster pre-dev cycles via Rapid Prototyping
ASPICE mastery — closing gaps with lean, systematic solutions
40% reduction in problem reports via high-integrity handling
Human scaling — strengthening strengths via proactive coaching
Global reach — coordinating hundreds of FTEs for Tier-1 OEMs

pillars.title: Growth Mindset
pillars.teaser: I've left a secure job, moved countries with zero language skills, lost money, failed a company, and sat the CFA in my 40s. Each one was a choice. Discover the six pivots that shaped who I am.

modal.html: You can read books about the Growth Mindset and believe in the theory, but you cannot advocate for it until you have been tested. To me, it isn't a soft skill — it is a calculated risk adventure. I allow myself and the people around me to make mistakes, but only once. A mistake is a data point. A repetition is a system failure.

If I have one talent, it is curiosity. I leverage it to make difficult decisions that others avoid. Every high-stakes pivot in my life was rewarded — though often preceded by significant work and suffering. That is the price of the adventure.

columns: The Pivot — left a secure job for a post-grad degree
The Reset — moved to Germany with zero language skills
The Crisis — started career in 2008 at below-average wage to enter the right field
The Gamble — invested every dime in real estate to learn the mechanics firsthand
The Failure — drop-shipping company that failed but taught me Business 101
The Grind — CFA in my 40s, balancing fatherhood and an ADAS career

modal.text: I am curious. I learn. I fail. I learn even more. Then, and only then, I grow. Growth is a system — and like every system, it must be examined continuously.

pillars.title: Finance
pillars.teaser: I didn't learn finance from books. I took an unpaid internship, renovated properties with my own hands, and acquired real estate across Germany. Then I passed the CFA — first attempt. Find out how I see money.

modal.html: I grew up in a classic Egyptian middle-class household. My father, a chemical engineer, taught me that security comes from a well-paying job and disciplined saving. I have a visceral hatred for waste — whether it is inefficient code or unnecessary luxury. But I realized that saving alone doesn't scale wealth. It only preserves it.

To understand the difference between rich and middle-class, I did what any engineer would do: I reverse-engineered the system. I didn't just read books — I took an unpaid internship at a real estate firm, renovated homes with my own hands, and eventually acquired multiple investment objects in Germany.
I passed the CFA Level 1 exam on the first attempt. I now view every R&D roadmap through the Investment Triangle:

items: Risk — what can go wrong, and what's the exposure?

modal.text: In the lab, money is not for spending — it is a resource to be allocated for maximum system growth.

pillars.title: Fitness & Nutrition
pillars.teaser: For five years I was the player who almost didn't make the team. Then I became a key scorer — scouted for the First Team. No talent required. Find out what relentlessness actually looks like over a decade.

modal.html: My mother always described me as "restless." I had a biological need to move — always. I started sports at age six and haven't stopped since. After five years of Karate and reaching a brown belt, I pivoted to Handball at age 11. That is where my real training began.

I'll be honest: I was the talentless player. Fit enough to make the team, making enough mistakes to be the target of frustration. I spent five years training relentlessly — gym sessions, basketball, tennis, volleyball, Judo, Kung Fu — all to find the missing edge. One day, the work paid off. I became a key high-scorer for the U18s and was scouted for the First Team.

modal.text: Consistency eventually overcomes a lack of initial calibration. I have tested this thesis across every domain of my life — and it has never failed.

modal.html: After moving to Germany, I became a Certified Fitness & Nutrition Coach specializing in Functional Training. Today, I apply the same high-performance logic to individuals and teams that I apply to my engineering projects.

items: Karate · Handball · Judo · Kung Fu · Basketball · Tennis · Volleyball
Talentless & Relentless — no shortcuts, no excuses, ever
